[13:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Playbook - 28 Day System"

 

NÚMEROS: 28 dias

 

VISUAL: Sistema de playbook detalhado mostrando abordagem multi-canal ao longo de 28 dias, incluindo email, LinkedIn, telefone, e outras táticas

 

[13:15] Now, the beautiful thing about this playbook is you can always add to it and you can always subtract to it. In fact, I've even seen clients and people that have done things like direct mail, direct letters, right? Sending a letter.

 

[13:30] Imagine sending a physical letter if you have the person's business address or address, right? In general, that's also effective. That can also work, right? So this is a key.

 

[13:45] Now, one thing to note is that we also don't want to, the worst thing is also, right, being a spammer or being known as a spammer. So we've also spaced it out in a way where it doesn't come across as super spammy.

 

[14:00] And the beautiful thing is as long as they responded, like what Felix said, either a yes, a no, or a neutral, this whole playbook is done.

 

[14:15] So let me give you an imperfect example. I'm talking to the CEO, like I told you about, the construction company. On day one, I sent him an email and he didn't open it. He didn't respond, right? I sent him an email at Dragon 100. No problem.

 

[14:30] Next day, I find him on LinkedIn and I add him, okay? He has an admin connection. That's cool. Next day, I send him a, I call him, I give him an FCC, and then I leave a LinkedIn message saying, hey, you know, John, I left you a voicemail. Any questions, get back to me, right?

 

[14:45] So I do that. Still no response. But on the fourth day, when I sent him that second email, right, second email, day four, and he responds, we're done. I have gone, I'm done. We move away from the playbook. We move to the next step.

 

[15:00] So it's only taken me up to day four to get a response, and then we don't have to worry about the rest of the days. So it's not like we're just spamming them for 28 days. As soon as we get some kind of response, the playbook is done. And that's the key, okay?

 

[15:15] James, Felix, anything else to add to this?

 

James: No, it's super, let me ask you this. Who likes automation? Who likes to have things work in their favor, leveraging their time?

 

[15:30] That's the beauty of the playbook. When it's successful, it stops. There's no turning it off, the faucet, or realigning. It's all about the system and leveraging. So you spend more time prospecting and even taking care of that client to bring them towards the finish line.

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Exemplo de subject line com emoji de foguete 🚀, personalização com nome da empresa/prospect

 

[27:30] Felix: All right, thank you. Let's move on to the next. So this is another example that I want to share with you that, again, leveraging a lot of the personalization, what we can do.

 

[27:45] And one thing I want to point out is, this is something that we found have a very high successful rate is if we always put the company name or the prospect name, first name on the subject line, this is really crucial, right?

 

[28:00] Because before you open the email, what do you see? All you see is the subject line first. So if there's a certain amount of customization on the subject line, you at least invite them to open the email, right?

 

[28:15] You don't want to have a boring subject line. Otherwise, all the work that you put in the body of the email will put into waste. So you want to make sure that your subject line also have some personalization in there. And we always, always make sure that we put it in there.

 

[28:30] Joel: So Felix, I got a question, Felix.

 

Felix: Yes.

 

Joel: So I see like a rocket, right? Like an emoji. Yeah, I was about to go into that. What is that? How has that been working?

 

[28:45] Felix: So this is something that we have been experimenting to put like a little emoji at the end. And this is something that we found quite interesting. Whenever we put an emoji at the end, it actually creates curiosity in prospect into opening the email as well.

 

[29:00] Where like, for example, like growing with a rocket, right? It add into a human psychology way of like, wow, like you can actually feel it. You can actually imagine how it looks like to grow, to actually going up.

 

[29:15] A lot of our email templates in the subject line, we'll put a little bit of a emoji in there.

 

Joel: So you're saying we've proven metrics that email with emoji subject lines actually get more open rates?

 

[29:30] Felix: I think it really depends. It depends on the prospect that you're talking to. It depends on the industry that we're talking to. But in general, yes, it does.

 

Joel: Wow, great.

 

[29:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: Email body com bold words e links coloridos

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Email mostrando palavras em negrito (scaling, growth, remote teams, social media, scalable business model) e link azul para Dragon 100

 

[30:00] Felix: Yeah, so another thing I want to highlight from this email is you would notice, actually, before I talk about what you would notice, I want everybody to point out, read this email a little bit and point out, other than the personalizations, other than the personalization that we have in the email, right?

 

[30:15] The first name, company name, the content owner, what else do we do in this email body? Type in the chat. What do you discover? What do you found?

 

[30:30] Bold words. Okay, everybody see that? Fantastic. Cold. Bold. Okay, I was like, cold? What do you mean? All right, cold, colored word. Dragon 100 is always blue. Okay, interesting. Someone found the second clue here. Scale revenue. That's right.

 

[30:45] All right, so that's right. The first thing I want to mention is bolding. Bolding, what that does, of course, you don't want to bold the whole thing. If you bold the whole thing, it just defeats the purpose.

 

[31:00] But what it means by bolding the word is something that's important, right? It highlights that something that's important, something that they need to catch their eyes. When I'm reading an email, if there's a relatively long email, what catches my eyes would be something that's a little bit different than the rest of the email.

 

[31:15] And bolding it would be one of the key that we use. So as you see, what we bold is scaling, right? Growth, remote teams, social media, scalable business model, right? These are the key things that Dragon 100 offers.

 

[31:30] So we want to highlight them and create that impression that these are important things that Dragon 100 is looking for, right? That's one of the things.

 

[31:45] Now, second things, another thing that everybody has discovered is why is Dragon 100 is blue, right? In terms of how it's shown in HubSpot is that it's actually a link where you can click onto that.

 

[32:00] And this gives an idea that if I click onto that, it will actually bring you to a website. And in this case, in this email, we're bringing the prospect to the Dragon 100 website.

 

[32:15] So we are already doing so much more just to ask like, hey, you want to discover more? But we also giving them the option to actually do their own research without them searching what Dragon 100 is. They can directly click onto that link.

 

[32:30] It creates curiosity on what you can do other than just reading the email. So another way why we put links in email is that we want to also track whether this email is effective.

 

[32:45] Meaning that we are always tracking the click rate. Meaning that if I have a link in the email, HubSpot can able to tell whether that prospect actually clicked on the link.

 

[33:00] So that's going to give me, like for Emerald, right? You guys love this. This gives us another number to lean on to go like, is this email effective? Are the prospects actually clicking on the email?

 

[33:15] That's another trick that we use to make sure that we have consistently metrics to look at to make sure like this is an effective email to look at.

 

[33:30] Joel: Exactly. Q, where are the Emeralds guys? Type in the chat. How many times when they click the link? That's right.

 

[33:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "HubSpot Sequence Results"

 

NÚMEROS:

71% taxa de abertura

VISUAL: Métricas de performance do HubSpot mostrando resultados impressionantes de uma sequência de email

 

[34:00] Felix: So these are the email templates that one of many that we want to share with you guys. And also like the key here is that this is just one of many and we are consistently revamping it. We're constantly looking at the numbers and making sure that these emails are very effective.

 

[34:15] And in fact, if Joey, if you can move on to the next slides. In fact, these numbers, I can tell you this, numbers doesn't lie. Numbers doesn't lie.

 

[34:30] This is one of the HubSpot sequence that we use and have created this result for us. Out of 80. By the way, the highlight there is one, one sequence, right? One, just one.

 

[34:45] Joel: Yeah, just one of the sequence that we recently tested, right? Out of 83 people. That's a pretty big sample size, right? 71% of the sequence is opened, right?

 

[35:00] Okay, let me give everybody a test. What does open rate means to you? Like if you have a high open rate, what does that mean? Type in the chat.

 

[35:15] Email was read. Subject line was good. Right. That's right. So it means that subject line is good. That means your subject line is catching the prospect eyes and they want to open it.

 

[35:30] Joel: Yeah, Vincent made a good point too, actually. It's actually landing in the inbox, not in the spam or the junk folder. That's a good point. That's correct too. Yeah, absolutely.

 

[35:45] By the way, I will share something really quick. That's a bit off. Well, not really off topic. It kind of relates. Yeah, go ahead, Joe.

 

[36:00] But just like what Vincent said, you could have the best email subject lines and copy and everything, right? The best copy in the world. [36:15] So for example, for us, we've got danlok.com, we've got dragon100.com, right? We've got highticketclosers.com, highticketsales.com.

 

[36:30] Those reputation, IP reputation, domain reputation, that's basically the number that's given by Google really affects your deliverability of your emails. And it will definitely affect your open rate as well.

[43:45] Get them to move, no action, no progress, action, okay, okay. Yeah, you guys, I think some of you are typing. It looks like Discord, you're still typing.

 

[44:00] Exactly, right? Delia, you want to get on the call, right? Because we are not going to qualify them through email, right? We're not going to say, all right, please answer 15 of my questions. What's your business revenue? You know, what's your business address?

 

[44:15] We're not going to do that in an email, right? Exactly. You want to move it to a call. And this is also another thing. I'm going to give you guys an extra bonus that's not here.

 

[44:30] Let's say we send this email to them, right? And for some reason, you know how, okay, you guys will probably experience this. I'm not the only one. How many of you, right, you get someone who's interested and you want to get them on a call, but they keep asking you questions back in the email.

 

[44:45] Like they never want to really talk about the call. They still email you back with more questions, right? You've got that, right? You definitely have got that. I'm not the only one, right?

 

[45:00] So it's really important that the way you respond to that is, you know what? Hey, John, you've got great questions. Now, it's just a give and take. I talked about it two weeks ago. It's a give and take.

 

[45:15] I might purposely answer one or two of their questions, but I'll say, you know what, John? It's a lot faster if I can answer all these for you on a call. It's not going to take long, 15 minutes, right?

 

[45:30] And you can have all the questions you want to answer. And then that's how you get them on that call, right? But especially with Emeralds who want to get all those answers from you through email, you've got to give them a bit, right?

 

[45:45] It's a give and take relationship. Answer some questions and then say, you know what? The rest of it is much better if I explain it on a call because besides, I don't want to have a miscommunication through email, right? I also want to make it clear. You can ask any clarifying questions. Let's jump on a call.

 

[46:00] James: Now, Joel, just to add on to that too, you know, we don't want to go too, how would you say, standoffish. We do want to at least answer one question. We don't want to be just jump on the call kind of situation.

 

[46:15] We do want to actually answer at least, you know, one or a couple questions. But don't answer all of them.

 

Joel: I definitely agree. Yeah, exactly what we talked about two weeks ago, right? The give and take relationship. Never just take, take, take, take, take. That's the number one thing that will get you a deal completely killed off for sure.

 

[46:30] All right, James?

NÚMEROS: 5,000% maior probabilidade, 5 minutos

 

VISUAL: Estatística impressionante sobre responder em 5 minutos, conceito de urgência e timing

 

[49:00] Joel: I'm going to add something too. This is one of Sifu's quotes that I absolutely love. I don't think, I don't know whether it's in HTC or whether it's in a circle, but Only Ben got it. Come on. Oh, there we go. We got. Cool.

 

[49:15] So, nope. There's a missing zero there. He's got it. You got it. Yep. She got it. You have 5,000% probability when you reach out to them within five minutes.

 

[49:30] Wait, wait, wait. But James, I mean, isn't that like super clingy? Like, you know, like super like, what do you call that? Like, yeah, like just super clingy. Like, why would I reach out to them so fast? Am I like desperate or something?

 

[49:45] James: Well, it's not the fact that we're desperate, but we're wanting to speak to them when they have their mind in the right position. That they have the intent to want to know more.

 

[50:00] They want to be able to figure out how this product and or service fits their needs. So, why not answer their questions right away and be that go-giving individual to fulfill their questions, to provide them the answers that they seek.

 

[50:15] Joel: And here's the truth, you know, because when I first did that, when I first saw this about a year ago, I kind of thought like, well, you know what? It could be, right? We don't want to be desperate, right?

 

[50:30] I was like, you know what? But you know, when I actually had that done to me, like this done to me when I submitted, you know, in Team Dan Lok, right? We try a lot of different tools, different services out.

 

[50:45] So, there was one day I submitted a form for a tool, but I forgot what the tool is. And literally the guy called me in like two minutes. Now, not for one second did I think they're desperate, not for one second was I like, what the heck? This guy's so clingy. It did not even cross my mind.

 

[51:00] As soon as I picked up the phone and I said hello, and he said, hey, you know, it's Michael from, I don't know, flyapp.com. I'm like, oh, great. I've got a question for you.

 

[51:15] Or like, hey, you know what? Yeah, wow. I just submitted my application two minutes ago. You guys are really on top of it. Like, not one second did it cross my mind about that whole desperation clingy thing.

 

[51:30] So, it also showed me that my human mind, my mind is also a limiting belief. It's also, right, preconceived notions that may not hold true. Again, like what Aaron said, it's your projections of your values. We think too much.

 

[51:45] James: Yeah, it really is. Like, don't overthink it. Don't say, oh, you know, I'm too desperate, blah, blah, blah. I got to wait. You know what? I can't reach out in five minutes. I got to wait five hours. Like, why? Who sets those rules? Why do you set those rules for your own brain, right?

 

[52:00] So, that's what I had to go through myself. And the last bullet point, you know, did that provide clarity in regards of, you know, when that individual reached back to you, Joel, did that affirm your why on why you were interested or why you thought this product or service was a necessity?

 

[52:15] Joel: 100%. I mean, we just naturally went into the conversation. There was no awkwardness. There was no weird feeling. I honestly didn't even think about the whole calling me back right away thing until many days later.

 

[52:30] I was like, wait, you know what? This company actually probably went through HubSpot, and they really know how to do it right because they got me while I was hot. Like what you said, James, I just submitted the form, right?

[60:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Why Gatekeepers Are Hostile"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Razões para hostilidade de gatekeepers - spam, mentiras, táticas enganosas de vendedores

 

[61:00] All right, now this one is something that I've got to hammer on again and again and again, and something that Sifu even told me to touch on again, right? Because believe it or not, until today, we have still got complaints from different business owners, different influencers about HTC students going out in the marketplace, right, and spamming people and being annoying.

 

[61:15] And yeah, but the truth of it, it just never ends, right? It's like we got to give constant reminders, but the reality doesn't end. And not surprisingly, even from my own community, imagine now the world, the world of salespeople and sales reps.

 

[61:30] Now you look at this, why are we not surprised that gatekeepers are still hostile, right, towards outside salespeople? Because again, a lot of sleazy, salesy, green monster trickery outright lies to reach the decision maker, right?

 

[61:45] Now, some of the things I've seen in the past and I've heard and I've actually experienced myself, some of the worst things, I'll give you some examples what not to do pretty much.

 

[62:00] I've seen sales reps, when they get a gatekeeper, they pretend to be like a customer, like they want to buy something. Hey, you know, I got to speak to your boss because I want to buy something. And then they get on the call with the boss and they pitch the boss something. Can you imagine that? Right?

 

[62:15] Yeah, of course, you got through the gatekeeper, but you pretty much started your whole relationship on a lie. Exactly, John. Right? It's like green monster crap.

 

[62:30] Or, hey, you know, I got to speak to your boss because I want to complain about your service. And the gatekeeper says, oh, of course, that's a very important thing. And then they go and then they, hey, you know, John, how would you like to buy my snake oil, right, for $2.99? Right?

 

[62:45] You know what I'm saying? Like, is that kind of horrible, horrible lie? So, and this has become almost like a stereotype, right? It's become almost like a, I don't even know what to call it.

 

[63:00] It's like, you know, that's when people say, what's the first thing that comes to your mind when you think salesperson? All right? Then they think, oh, snake oil, slimy, right, full of crap, right? It's because of this. It's because of crap like that.

 

[63:15] And that's why it's ultra high ticket closers. We never use these techniques. You will never hear us say, oh, yeah, jump on the phone and just lie so you can get to the decision maker. We're never endorsing that. We're never going to share that.

What do you think is the worst case scenario, right, for let's say you speak to the decision maker, what's the worst case scenario? What's the worst thing that can happen?

 

[64:00] Just some of you guess in the chat, like what's the worst thing? You guys say no, okay. Is that the worst thing? They say no, hang up. Hang up. No, no, no. Okay, okay. They lose their job. Adam's getting far. Yeah, you said don't ever call again.

[64:30] You will never, ever, ever have a chance to get to the decision maker, ever. That's the worst case scenario. And by the way, not only for you, but because of my actions, right, your actions, it affects the rest of our company's reputation.

[66:30] Of course, the latter. Everyone wants this, right? The best case scenario. So let's go deeper into how can we get here, right? How do we actually do this? Okay?

 

[66:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "4 Types of Gatekeepers"

 

NÚMEROS: 4 tipos identificados

 

VISUAL:

Front Desk Receptionist

Automated System/Robots

[Outros dois tipos não especificados neste trecho]

 

[67:00] All right. Before that, James, tell us about this.

[67:15] I guarantee, James, everyone here in this room has at least met one of these. I'm sure. Each one. All four. Front desk receptionist. My wife. Each one. I don't like the automated ones. Yeah, those are, they're tough.

[68:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Front Desk Receptionist Profile"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Características detalhadas:

Ambiente caótico e ocupado

Múltiplas interrupções constantes

Conhecimento médio da estrutura da empresa

Propósito: Bloquear requisições desnecessárias

 

[68:15] Let me introduce the front desk receptionist. All right. It's not really that royal, but. So front desk receptionist. What is going on with front desk receptionist, right?

 

[68:30] They are the one who you imagine yourself going to a bank, right? Going to any company, you'll have the front desk, right? They will be the one who's sitting there, right? And they're in that environment where people are coming in and out, right? Getting a lot of different phone calls.

 

[68:45] They're in a high level chaotic environment. There's so many things going around them, super busy and consistently, constantly interrupt, right? They have a lot of like different people calling them to tell them to do things.

 

[69:00] But usually they will only have maybe like a medium level knowledge about how the company is structured, right? Good enough to know, you know, who is the right guy to direct the calls to or whether or not.

 

[69:15] But they usually have, you know, medium level knowledge, right? They kind of know who they should talk to or who they should not talk to. And what is their purpose? Their purpose is to block unnecessary requests.

 

[69:30] So they are trying to not really having somebody to just randomly go through them and waste any other people's time in the company, right? But they're very having a little time. They literally just, you know, the first 10 seconds, they will have to jump on the different things.

 

[69:45] So that's usually how the characteristics of a front desk receptionist would do. And really key, I want to point out the word underlined, they really are here to block unnecessary requests.

 

[70:00] Can someone tell us why is that the case? Why is that their job? Why do they care? Actually, who has ever worked as a front desk receptionist before? I bet there's people that have done that. I've kind of done similar stuff. Yeah, Stanley, you have.

[70:30] Yeah, right? Joshua, you don't want to pass them up to your boss, right? Exactly, right? And why? But why? Why is that, Joshua? Tell us. Why would you not pass a telemarketer to your boss?

 

[70:45] Yeah, waste of his time? Exactly. And first of all, yeah, Rich, exactly, right? Imagine if you're the front desk receptionist that keeps passing all these waste of time people...

[74:30] They basically, only when you get a human or in a queue, then it calls you back so that you can pick up and talk to a real human. It's pretty badass.

 

Joel: It is pretty badass. So yeah, gethuman.com. Definitely try that.

 

[74:45] And here's a fun tip. When you have to dial certain numbers to get to certain selections, press 1 for this, press 2 for that, press 3. If you hit the number and you have to enter in a series of other numbers, you can use the pound key or for the younger individuals, the hashtag key.

 

[75:00] You can use the hashtag key or the pound key to be able to enter your selection and get to the next step faster instead of having to hear the repeated numbers.

 

[75:15] Yep, leverage AI. Absolutely. Welcome in. Absolutely.

[85:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "What NOT To Do with Gatekeepers"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Lista de comportamentos a evitar:

Mentir

Ser típico vendedor escorregadio

Esconder informações

Fazer exigências

Começar relacionamento em conflito

 

[85:45] All right. Cool. Here. This is what not to do. Now, before we talk about things that we should do and things we should, you know, some of the best practices and tips, right? We always want to start with what not to do because I love it, right?

 

[86:00] There's always things we should avoid. Things you definitely not want to do with gatekeepers. Obviously, we talked about a couple. Don't lie to them. Don't start your relationship off on a lie. Don't be a typical slimy salesperson, right?

 

[86:15] Now, here's the thing. And this is where we've seen a lot of people fall as well and get into a lot of issues is trying to withhold information from gatekeepers just because they are not decision makers.

 

[86:30] And that also goes down to the respect, right? It goes down to you want to treat the gatekeeper like they're a buyer. You really do. You want to treat the gatekeeper like a key decision maker.

 

[86:45] And in fact, they very well could be a key decision maker that you're not aware of or at the very least, they could be a big supporter to the key decision maker.

 

[87:00] So yeah, by not revealing information to them, you are indirectly almost insulting their intelligence. Exactly, John. Or you're insulting their capabilities to understand and receive that information. Right?

 

[87:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Making Demands = Starting a Fight"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Exemplos de linguagem exigente a evitar, conceito de "arm wrestling" no início do relacionamento

 

[87:30] And next one. This is a big one. It goes back to the give and take that I talked about, right? Now, making demands. Let me give you some examples.

 

[87:45] Hey, Jake, I've got enough questions from you. Let me speak to John. Hey, you know what? I've answered your questions, Michelle. Let me just speak to John, please. Right? That's what I mean by making demands of your own. Right?

 

[88:00] Now, obviously, that comes across as what would you guys say that is, right? What are some terms you use to describe that? Low respect? Yep. Definitely low respect. Arrogant. Exactly. A-hole, right? Too demanding. Pushy. Exactly. Right?

 

[88:15] And it's sort of like basically you're saying, yeah, you're not important to me. Right? We definitely don't want to go there.

 

[88:30] And then the biggest part here, this is the whole give and take concept. The entirety, the basis of the give and take relationship. You don't want them to feel like they're wrestling with you.

 

[88:45] Why would you start? No. It's one thing to start the relationship on a lie. But why would you start a relationship in an arm wrestle, in a wrestling match? Why would you start a relationship fighting with each other or trying to show, you know, who's boss, right?

 

[89:00] Like, I'm boss, right? Let me through because I'm the important guy here, right? We definitely don't want to start off on that relationship as well.

 

[89:15] I like that, Aaron. That's a great statement right there. Diffuse, don't escalate, right? Or inform, right, and then politely make a request rather than demand. 100%. That's super, super, super key.

 

[89:30] Okay. James.

 

[89:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Best Practice: Reach KDM Directly When Possible"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Estratégia ideal - contatar Key Decision Maker diretamente, evitar gatekeeper quando possível, mas respeitar quando necessário

 

[90:00] James: Yeah. So when doing research, you know, finding the right key decision maker, you know, it can be a perilous journey. But when you do find them, you know, it's ideal to contact them.

 

[90:15] You know, if you do get the opportunity to be able to reach them directly, you know, if it may be contacting the key decision maker around the schedule of the gatekeeper.

 

[90:30] But essentially, if that is not possible, we do need to engage them with that level of respect. You know, as Joel was mentioning earlier, we don't want to just go in guns a-blazing. Hey, I want to talk to somebody who's important enough and not you. That's just completely the wrong way to do it.

 

[90:45] And, you know, to be able to be in contact with the KDM or the key decision maker, you know, it's very, very ideal to be able to make it a mutual respect to be able to go forward to the next step.

 

[91:00] Now, in regards of being able to engage them, we also need to know what they do and how they are. Hey, kid, who calls the shots here?

 

[91:15] So we're going to go ahead and go to the next slide.

[93:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Holy Grail: Referrals"

 

NÚMEROS: 100 gatekeepers vs 100 decision makers

 

VISUAL: Conceito de referência como método mais eficaz, comparação entre falar com gatekeepers vs decision makers

 

[93:15] Here's one tip I would say in regards to what is great. The most classic and the most effective way to avoid the gatekeeper is pretty much getting a referral. All right, that's like the holy grail of sales, right?

 

[93:30] If you can get a referral to the decision maker, then you can get past the gatekeeper, right? The question is, well, how do you get that referral, right? And there are many ways to do it.

 

[93:45] Obviously, one is if you partner up with the gatekeeper, right? You build rapport. He or she likes you, respects you. That's already a referral right there.

 

[94:00] Then you can also obviously go to events, right? This is the stuff we learn in HTC class. Go to events, right? Talk to different LinkedIn mutual connections, right? Network around.

 

[94:15] So that is still the most effective way, right, of getting to the decision maker. And that's something that we should always focus on as ultra high-tech closers, right?

 

[94:30] Because if you had a choice, would you rather speak to 100 gatekeepers or 100 decision makers? All right, it's a no-brainer, right? It's a no-brainer, right? You want a decision maker.

 

[94:45] James: This is James. Yeah, absolutely. Thank you.

 

[95:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Art of War Principle: Know Your Enemy"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Citação "To know your enemy, you must become your enemy" - aplicação aos gatekeepers, exercício de visualização

 

[95:15] When it comes down to, you know, the art of war, I have it actually sitting right above my head in my bookshelf that I just installed. And, you know, to know your enemy, you must become your enemy.

 

[95:30] You need to think exactly what they would want to hear from an individual who's speaking to them. If they're aggressive, if not, you need to understand what they're taking, what's incoming to them. So what I would say is put yourself in their shoes.

 

[95:45] Joel: Yeah. Right? This is an exercise, right? Let's do it together, right? Let's do a visualization exercise. So, you know, close your eyes. Imagine right now you are a gatekeeper.

 

[96:00] And you can choose. You can choose which one you want to be, right? Close your eyes. You could be an executive assistant. You could be a front desk receptionist. You could be one of the switchboard operators.

 

[96:15] Heck, if you want to be a robot, be a robot, right? Put yourself in a robot's shoes. Just joking, right? But put yourself in one of the other three, the human ones, right?

 

[96:30] Just from that exercise alone is already going to reveal to you a lot of do's and don'ts. I guarantee you, okay? So let's do this, right? Let's take just 30 seconds.

 

[96:45] Just focus on being a gatekeeper. And you will think naturally of all the things that you would do or you would not do, right, when you're speaking to a salesperson or closer, right?

 

[97:00] So imagine you're a gatekeeper. You're speaking to a closer. What are some things that you wish or would hope that the person does not do or does to you?

 

[97:15] All right, and then we'll come back to this course and we'll put some of that in the chat, okay? So visualize that. I'm a gatekeeper speaking to a closer. These are the things I wish the closer did or did not do. And then we'll come back.

 

[97:30] Okay, I see a lot already. I see some, actually, John and Adam, perfect. They will speak slowly and clearly. Fantastic. That's what we want, yes.

 

[97:45] They'd grade, third grade, fourth grade words, you want to be concise and have finesse. Now, you don't want to overload by providing every single bullet point value proposition that your product or service can bring.

 

[98:00] You know, you can find out what is important to them. What is important to that company?

 

[98:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Gatekeeper Reality: They're NOT Waiting For Your Call"

 

NÚMEROS: 2-3 parágrafos mencionados (tempo de atenção)

 

VISUAL: Imagem conceitual de gatekeeper ocupado, não esperando ansiosamente por ligações de vendas, múltiplas interrupções

 

[98:30] Now, if we think of it too, when you're coming in, getting on the phone and talking to the gatekeeper, let's go to the next slide, they're not waiting by bait and breath for your call.

 

[98:45] James: Did we say, you know, this is a question, I mean, why do we not have to reveal which company we represent? Like, didn't, you know, we learn in class a couple months ago, we need to say, hey, you know, I'm from dragon100.com, you know, I'm like, is that not important?

 

[99:00] Well, it's not that it's not important, but what it comes down to is with all the confusing lingos, acronyms, and as well as words that we're comfortable with by being familiar with the product or service, you know, what we're wanting to do is be straightforward on what value that we can provide.

 

[99:15] This is what we do. We help companies that want to go from six to seven figures or seven to eight. They are not waiting for your call. They're not looking at the phone, all right, when's that next salesperson coming through?

 

[99:30] They're going to be busy. They're going to have all those salespeople coming in with all that input that comes into their brain being very, well, not too vague again. Being vague is just fine because once you go to the pitch, like every other salesperson, you know, they go through about two, three paragraphs and they're rambling through, you know, do you think they're listening?

 

[99:45] You know, I think they're going to be doing what the next slide shows. I don't think they're going to be listening at all. I think they're going to be letting you speak kind of like when you're on the phone on some of those movies when mom is calling and she's rambling on and you kind of take the phone away from the receiver, go back to it.

 

[100:00] Oh, she's still talking. All right, sweet. And then taking away the phone again, waiting for you to finish or even just hang up right on you. That's the kind of situation they're in.

 

[100:15] So we're deep diving back into, you know, what is it like to be in their shoes?

 

[100:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Opening Examples - What NOT To Do vs Better Way"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado: ❌ "Hi, this is John from Dragon 100. I want to speak with Amy." ✅ "I'm calling from Vancouver, Canada. Is Amy available right now?"

 

[100:45] So let's look at an example. Let's do some quick role plays really fast. Okay. So what not to do, James?

 

James: Hi, this is John from Dragon 100. I want to speak with Amy.

 

[101:00] Joel: All right. Why is this not a good opening for a gatekeeper when you're speaking to the gatekeeper? Let's get some engagement here. Why is this an act? Why is there a big X across this whole statement?

 

[101:15] Company. Just listen, don't care. Everyone does it. Company name, train like a robot. No respect. The word I. Tonality. Yep, tonality is big. Yes, yes.

 

[101:30] And someone, I'm looking for a word. What's it? Yes, Aaron. Exactly. Aaron's got the word. Demanding. You come on and you ask for something without even building any rapport.

 

[101:45] It's like the whole give and take. I just want to speak with Amy. I don't care about anything else. Just let me speak with Amy. This is not the way to start the relationship, start the call.

 

[102:00] Yeah, who do you think you are, right? Okay. Felix?

 

Felix: Better way. Listen, observe Felix's tonality, right? And also the actual two sentences. I'm calling from Vancouver, Canada. Is Amy available right now?

 

[102:15] Joel: Fantastic. Why? Now, first of all, I think that's a pretty good boom, right? I would say that's a boom. A mini boom. Yeah. Is that when the boom? Yes.

 

[102:30] Why is that much better? I haven't seen your customer support. Perfect tonality. Yes, I would agree. It's more caring. It's more respectful, definitely. Right? Definitely.

 

[102:45] Actually, let's do a quick little, you know, part of role play. The other thing as well, you know, I find this as a very important skill as a closer, right?

 

[103:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Chess Game Mentality - Predict Next Moves"

 

NÚMEROS: 3, 4, 5 passos à frente mencionados

 

VISUAL: Conceito de closing como jogo de xadrez, prever 3-5 movimentos à frente, antecipar respostas do gatekeeper

 

[103:15] Now, I always, when I used to do the coaching sessions for the B2C division like a year ago, we always did talk about, I always talk about the whole, my journey as a closer and then understanding how closing works.

 

[103:30] It's kind of like a spider web, right? You pull in different parts of a call and you all link it back to that center of the spider web, which is the strongest part.

 

[103:45] And a critical skill of being a closer is being able to predict almost the future, predict the next couple steps, right? If you can see three, four, five steps ahead of the call, if you can predict what the prospect's going to say, what the gatekeeper's going to say back, you can already plan out your next couple moves really, really well.

 

[104:00] So let's practice, let's practice that as a chess game. Exactly. Let's practice our chess skills. What do you think the gatekeeper is going to say next?

 

[104:15] When Felix said, hey, I'm calling from Vancouver, Canada, is Amy available right now? What is the most likely next statement?

 

[104:30] Yes. Yes, you guys got it. What do you need Amy for? What's this about? Yes, exactly. Yes, you guys got it, right? Put yourself in the gatekeeper's shoes. Exactly.

 

[104:45] Okay. What is this call about? Fantastic. All right, and we'll answer that. We'll answer that in the next slide. Is it the next slide? Let me check. Yeah, we will talk about this now. We're going to answer that. James?

 

[105:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ask and Answer Questions Strategy"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Estratégia de fluxo conversacional - perguntas e respostas balanceadas, ser conciso, não dominar a conversa

 

[105:15] James: Yeah. So when it comes down to the questions and answers that you're going to be, or the interchange between you and the gatekeeper, ask and answer questions. Be concise. Don't go overboard.

 

[105:30] You don't want to take over the call because as the picture has shown earlier, they're not going to listen when it comes down to the talk track if it's too long or pitchy.

 

[105:45] And what you want to do is be able to provide the opportunity to continue with that conversation. You don't want it to be shut down. You don't want it to be stopped immediately. You want to get to the next step. And that's where the chess match comes in.

 

[106:00] Joel: So perfect. Let's do a quick thing, right? So like you all said, right, how do you know Amy? Why are you calling for? Why do you want to talk to Amy?

 

[106:15] Okay, I'm going to ask this. I'm going to throw this back to you guys. Now you are the closer speaking to the gatekeeper. You just said that first statement and I said to you, why do you want to speak to Amy? Or what is this call about?

 

[106:30] How would you respond? What's your next chess move? All right, comment in Discord. We're going to wait and we're going to pick the best answers.

 

[106:45] When the gatekeeper says to you, why do you want to speak to Amy? Or what is this call about? How would you move step number two?

[107:15] Okay, we'll just give you guys a couple seconds because remember short and concise as possible. I don't know. I don't know. Just put me through please for you. I don't think that's going to fly.

 

[107:30] Ah, that's actually your answer. I could give you 30 foot. Yep. Okay. Yeah, people all thought it was a joke actually. Adam, I think you've made enough puns tonight. No one actually took that seriously.

 

[107:45] Oh, that's actually a direct answer. Okay, yeah. I could give you, yeah, that works. That works. I would, yes, that's actually a great response. I would give you 30,000 foot if that helps. Yes.

 

[108:00] Ah, John, I understand that she's looking for X and I was calling to help. Okay, not bad. Let's see. I would like to talk to you. Ah, the referral one is pretty bad. If you have that referral, that's always great for sure.

[109:00] Okay, James. I think we have some good UHTC people in here. Yes, they're getting it fast. Absolutely.

 

[109:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Why Avoid Revealing Company Name Initially"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Razões estratégicas:

Prospect pode ter sido contactado antes por outro membro da equipe

Nome da empresa pode causar shutdown imediato

Múltiplos fatores que podem causar desatenção

 

[109:30] You know, it's avoiding revealing your company name in the beginning. There's going to be a couple different means to this. You know, one of them could be is this could be a prospect that's been previously called from another individual from your team.

 

[109:45] Mentioning the name of the company could shut it down. When it comes down to also names of companies, it can be other factors where they're not going to pay attention to what you're saying.

 

[110:00] Why would you think that? I'm going to actually ask you guys in the chat. Why would you think that avoid revealing your company name is kind of a big deal?

 

[110:15] So, William, I've heard that Amy has had some problems about X. Correct, that's going to be kind of teaming with, you know, maybe the wrong individual called them from that same...

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Exemplo de resposta do gatekeeper tentando encerrar conversa, técnica de backpedaling e recuperação

 

[110:45] Like that's what I would go with for that response. If this is important, she'll get back to you when she's available. Anything else?

 

[111:00] Okay, great. So Shang is a perfect example, right? If the gatekeeper wants to sort of shut you off and get you away from the call, so let's say we say, hey, you know, my name is Joel from Canada. I sent Amy a message a while ago and it's a pretty important message that I'm following up with, right? May I please speak to Amy?

 

[111:15] And then Shang says, well, if it's important, she'll get back to you when she's available. Is there anything else I can help you with?

 

[111:30] Oh, you know, Shang, I don't mean to, I think I got off on the wrong foot here, Shang. So this is where I would, in a way, kind of apologize. You know, hi, Shang, I think I got off on the wrong foot here.

 

[111:45] What I actually mean was that the Amy, I sent Amy a message and it's been a couple of weeks now that she hasn't responded to. And I was wondering whether you could help me out. Maybe I got her email wrong or maybe I got, or maybe if you passed me a phone number that I could contact, right?

 

[112:00] So it's going in, and we will talk about backpedaling as well as taking control of a conversation, a call. We're going to show you how you could do that to regain control of the conversation, right? So it's that kind of finesse that you've got to implement.

 

[112:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "When Gatekeepers Ask For Company Name"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Estratégia de give-and-take quando perguntado diretamente sobre empresa, como redirecionar com finesse

 

[112:30] Some gatekeepers will ask for the company, especially executives. Yeah, so the whole company thing is, again, it's common sense, right? If they really say, literally say, hey, I want to know your company, don't just be like, no, or no, or keep being around the bush.

 

[112:45] Just tell them the freaking company, right? If it's like, it's the whole give and take thing, but it's also common sense. It's like, if they really want to know it, then sure, right? They can know it.

 

[113:00] But at the same time, at the same time, you always want to redirect the finesse. Move that conversation forward. Because if you just said, oh, well, the company is Danlok Organization, and that's it, now you're going to put them in a much more inquisitive state where they're going to keep asking a lot of questions to kind of interrogate you.

 

[113:15] So I might say, yeah, so Adam, so the company is Danlok Organization. And I said before, we help companies with a brick and mortar business scale more effectively and build remote teams. So I was wondering whether Amy was interested in this, which you happen to know, right?

 

[113:30] Then I throw the ball back at them. I throw the question back at them. It's just, I just, that's what I do, right? I throw the question back at them.

 

[113:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Conversation Flow = Questions & Answers Balance"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Conceito de fluxo conversacional natural - alternância equilibrada entre perguntas e respostas, evitar interrogatório

 

[114:00] And it's always, see, this is the key. It's always a conversation when there's a flow between question and answers, question and answers. Like Philip says, exactly. You don't want them to be the one controlling the conversation.

 

[114:15] Now it ends up, you're being interviewed or interrogated by them, right? We've done nothing wrong. We've done nothing wrong at this point. We don't need them to interrogate us this way. You know what I mean?

 

[114:30] So when the conversation is free flowing, they ask a question, I ask a question. They ask a question, I give an answer. I ask a question, they give an answer. I ask a question, that's how it should be. It should be natural.

 

[114:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Reality Check: One 'No' ≠ Game Over"

VISUAL: História inspiradora de persistência - Sifu ligou 13+ vezes para Linda (gatekeeper) até conseguir falar com Big Dan

[115:15] Does that mean it's game over and we just give up and we just, you know, like, yeah, you might not get through today. Okay, perfect. Example, the whole story with Shifu wanting to talk to Big Dan and the gatekeeper, Linda, right? Was it Linda or Laura?

 

[115:30] I think Adam corrected James just now. Linda, exactly. Shifu called, what, 13 times or more. Just because on day one, the gatekeeper doesn't want to transfer me, it doesn't mean it's done.

 

[115:45] And it's the same thing as we talked about two weeks ago, right? Just because it doesn't happen then and there doesn't mean the deal is completely off the table.

 

[116:00] Then it's where we come back, you know what? Amy, I think I caught you at a, oh, sorry. Gatekeeper, I caught you at a bad time. Maybe Amy's busy. I'll call back in 24 hours, right? Have a good day. Take care, right?

[117:30] They put you in a blacklist only if you act very inappropriately or in this case, you act like a typical salesperson who's super, super annoying, right?

 

[117:45] There could be a point though, David, right? There could be a point where even though you're being super respectful, super polite, and they still might blacklist you. You know what? That goes back to the entire thing of, you know, hey, thank you, next, right? Next.

 

[118:00] Like Sifu says, next, you move on, right? You've got a hundred, if not thousands of leads in your pipeline. You're not going to cry over one lead, right?

 

[118:15] If you've done your best, you've done your part, and you still got blacklisted. Now, there's always things you can take from it. You can always learn from it. Why do you think they blacklisted you? Was it because the gatekeeper was just being mean? Or did you do something wrong, right? You've got to analyze that.

 

[118:30] Why was I put on a blacklist? Now, if you're put on a blacklist more than one time with different companies, then you probably want to figure out why am I always put on a blacklist?

[119:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Pattern Interrupts - Taking a Step Back"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Técnicas de pattern interrupt para resetar conversação quando está indo mal:

"Maybe we got off on the wrong foot"

"Let me take a step back"

"Would it be a waste of time to discuss..."

 

[119:30] James: Yeah, so kind of where Joel was reasserting the position and resetting the board, you know, verbally taking a step backwards. You know, if you're ever on the back foot when you're in the conversation, always redirect, always bring it forward.

 

[119:45] And there's going to be pattern interrupts that you can put into place to be able to make that a point. Just as a conversation as it would be with individuals from face to face.

 

[120:00] You can use that pattern interrupt to be able to get back to the point or to be able to bring forward what you're wanting to provide as value.

 

[120:15] So what are a couple of ways to say it? You know, maybe we got off on the wrong foot. How would you say that is as a pattern interrupt?

 

[120:30] All right, James, you want to do this with your tonality?

 

James: Yeah, the next one. So actually, let me take a step back and tell you who I am and why I'm calling.

 

[120:45] Joel: Great. Awesome. And, you know, I'll say, okay, well, fair. Well, James, go ahead. Tell me who you are and why you're calling for Amy, right? That's the response.

 

[121:00] James: Yeah, so essentially what happens is you're actually, you know, even painting the picture of taking a step back that, you know, you are aware of where you are at. And that is a respectable behavior when it comes down to a conversation.

 

[121:15] You know, stepping back from, you know, where you were beforehand, that shows one maturity and as well as that you're a real person. You're not just going off of a script. You're not just trying to plug through. You're not tired just going through the phone and just trying to burn leads. You're actually going with intent.

 

[121:30] Joel: Exactly. And with the right tonality. All right, I'll do this one. So, hey, gatekeeper, perhaps we got off the wrong foot. Would it be a waste of time to discuss scaling your business to see if we could help?

 

[121:45] Yeah, nice. Pretty good, right? Perhaps we got off on the wrong foot. Now, would it be a waste of time to discuss scaling your business to see if we could help Amy? Right, that'd be something I say.

 

[122:00] And then, yeah, you know, they probably won't say, oh, yeah, you're a waste of time. They won't be that direct, but they'll probably ask you some more questions, which is fine.

 

[122:15] It's taking a step back again, right? In the art of war too, it's part of combat strategy, right? Sometimes you got to hunker down, take a couple steps back to move five steps forward. And it's part of strategy, right? James?

[123:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "GOLD TECHNIQUE: Pre-Qualifying Questions That Gatekeepers Can't Answer"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Técnica avançada - fazer perguntas que o gatekeeper não consegue responder, forçando transferência para nível superior ou KDM

 

[123:30] And one of the last couple of techniques here is, this is pretty gold. But to pull this off, you've got to definitely make sure that the tonality is right and you're also presenting this properly, which we'll also show you.

 

[123:45] And the key here is if you ask a question that the gatekeeper does not know how to answer. Meaning the question might go over their head and they might automatically defer to higher or they might automatically defer to the decision maker because they feel like it's something that the higher levels, the higher ups can answer.

 

[124:00] In most cases, decision maker, right? So when you ask pre-qualifying questions to get you further, if they don't know the answer, you will have a higher chance of reaching the KDM.

 

[124:15] And if you do, so Felix, you want to go ahead and take this one?

 

[124:30] Felix: All right. So that's one of the reasons why I'm reaching out because Amy will have that information. So what's the best way to get in contact with her?

 

[124:45] Joel: So this goes back to redirecting with finesse, right? Now, it's a case where if you're asking a key question, like for example, I can think of one right off the bat is let's say we're talking about Dragon 100 and it's about growing a remote team, having a scalable business model as well as having social media reach, right?

 

[125:00] I might ask the gatekeeper this. So Mr. Gatekeeper, right? So like I mentioned, we help brick and mortar businesses scale and part of doing that is through having leverageable remote teams as well as a very scalable business model.

 

[125:15] Would you happen to know whether the current business model in your organization is pretty scalable, right? That might be a question.

 

[125:30] Now, that might get me to the decision maker because the response might be, hey, you know what? I'm not too sure exactly. I think Amy or whoever could answer that for you.

 

[125:45] Or what percentage of your company right now, organization right now has a remote setting, right? Is there anyone at all, right? And depending on how big the organization is, they would have to move that to a different person to answer that question, right?

 

[126:00] If they don't know how to answer that question, you would increase your chances of getting to the next person. And that's kind of the basis behind this, right?

 

[126:15] Okay. Looks like we are, wow, the chat has gone awfully quiet. You guys all still there? Are we asleep? Are we doing okay?

 

[126:30] Yes. Oh, now everyone's back. Taking notes. Perfect, perfect.

 

[126:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "BONUS TECHNIQUE: The Two Things Technique"

 

NÚMEROS: 2 coisas

 

VISUAL: Técnica avançada de pattern interrupt:

Pedir ao gatekeeper para escrever 2 coisas específicas em nota

Colocar nota em lugar inusitado (cadeira, monitor, geladeira)

Captura atenção do KDM de forma única

 

[127:00] So one last thing that I want to share, this is really the final thing for tonight's class on gatekeeper. By the way, how many of you have found this pretty useful, pretty helpful so far?

 

[127:15] Now, looking back at the questions on Reddit before, right? Getting past brick wall gatekeepers and all that, right? Now, do you feel more equipped to go back and answer those questions?

 

[127:30] The promise we did in the beginning, taking it back now, this is where we are, right? At the point of class where we hope that you've gained some value and you can implement it in your next calls.

 

[127:45] It's very important because without the implementation, all this talk is not going to get us anywhere, right?

 

[128:00] So here's a bonus technique that I absolutely love and I've done this a bunch of times and it's a great pattern interrupt. And the right gatekeeper will help you, if they help you pull this off really well, it's phenomenal.

 

[128:15] By the way, this technique can only be applied when you've built that trust and that rapport with the gatekeeper, like anything else, and it's to grab the KDM's attention maker, okay?

 

[128:30] So this is key. I call it the two things technique. And actually I shared this with, back in the day we had the double O's, right? The double O's for the biz dev. We talk about the two things technique, right?

 

[128:45] It's basically this. It's actually two steps. So you tell the gatekeeper, Mrs. Gatekeeper, I know the KDM, Mr. Biz Owner is out right now or he or she is busy. Could you just please help me write down two things on a piece of paper for him, right?

 

[129:00] First, higher sales conversions. And second, higher quality clients. Just help me write that down on the piece of paper and let me know when you've done that, right? Please.

 

[129:15] And now this part, when you build the rapport, you've built the trust, they say, yeah, sure, why not? I'll be taking a note, right?

 

[129:30] And you say, this is really key. Don't just take a note that just says, Joe, go, my phone number, my email address. No, this is a pattern interrupt, not the typical notes, the very boring notes, right?

 

[129:45] This is literally, I just want you to write two things. Higher sales conversions. And second, higher quality clients. And now you can put my name, just say Joe and then my phone number, that's it. That's all you write on that piece of note, please.

 

[130:00] Yes. Fantastic. Now, here's the kicker. This part is really gold. Here's a huge pattern interrupt moment, okay? Here's really key.

 

[130:15] Now, if you don't mind, you know, Mr. Gatekeeper, I'm going to ask you to do something for me that's pretty unexpected, if you will. Instead of leaving it on his desk, because I'm probably guessing John's desk is pretty busy with a lot of papers and documents.

 

[130:30] Could you please leave the note on his chair, right? Exactly. Chang knows where I'm getting at this. Could you please leave this note on his microwave door? Could you please leave his note on the mouse, on his monitor screen, right? Could you please stick this note on his mini fridge?

 

[130:45] Somewhere where it's going to catch the attention of the KDM, right? And it's not in a place where people normally leave notes.

 

[131:00] Plus, you have two things, higher sales conversions and, right, what's the second one? More clients. And you put your name and number, and that's it. Boom. That's the technique.

 

[131:15] All right. So this is something that's very effective. But, again, to execute this, you've got to be able to build that rapport with the gatekeeper first.

 

[131:30] Now, when the gatekeeper is able to help you out, willing to help you out, help you take notes, this is a perfect – so this I want you to practice. I want you to, in a role place, right, get to that point and then ask.

 

[131:45] So, like, when we do the role place in a few minutes, we're going to call some individuals who do it. If the gatekeeper says, hey, you know what, John is not available right now, would you like me to help you take a note? I want you to execute this as well as you can.

 

[132:00] So practice this, right? Okay, you know what? Felix, thanks so much. If you could help me do me a favor, what kind of notepads do you guys have in your office, right?

 

[132:15] Oh, we've got – I guess we've got sticky notes. We've got paper. Do you happen to have a pink sticky note? A pink one? Yeah, a pink one. Yeah, sure.

 

[132:30] If you don't mind, this is kind of funny, but could you just write down two things on that pink sticky note? Okay, sure. All right, and then you go over there. So first this, second that. And then just put my name on my phone number.

 

[132:45] And then you go, let's put this somewhere that's super unexpected, right? That's how you end. Now, that's how you get the attention of the decision maker, right? We go from there.

 

[133:00] Okay, did you guys like that bonus technique? I think that was pretty new for quite a lot of you. I think that's pretty – yeah, it's a pretty eye-opening pattern interrupt. It's definitely very badass, right?

 

[133:15] So fantastic. Okay, so Donna, I think we have time to do probably two or three role plays.

 

[133:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "ROLE PLAY TIME - Volunteers Needed"

 

NÚMEROS: 2-3 role plays planejados, 100+ pessoas na call

 

VISUAL: Chamada para voluntários de role play, requisitos: boa qualidade de áudio, sem ruído de fundo, disposição para participar

 

[133:45] So if we could have people raise their hand, let's go select some individuals, preferably people who haven't gone two weeks ago. So we can rotate some new people.

 

[134:00] So right now I'm looking for you guys to put up your hands. Right now I only see Bogdan. He's the only one with his hand up. If you do want a role play, please make sure you have good audio quality. Background noise is pretty clear and be willing to go for it.

 

[134:15] So if no one new is going to volunteer, we're going to select people from the last two weeks anyways, right? Right now, wow, we don't have a lot of people actually who wants to volunteer. I'm surprised.

 

[134:30] It's always the same people, guys. It's Bogdan, Adam, and William, and John. Seriously, guys? We've got over 100 people on the call, yet we're getting – okay, finally.

 

[134:45] So we got Rich. I know. Let Rich go first, and then we can go back to the ones that have done this before. So Rich. Yeah, Rich. I would like you to talk. Are you there?

 

[135:00] Rich: Hello, can you hear me?

 

Joel: Loud and clear.

 

[135:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "ROLE PLAY #1 - Rich"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Setup do role play:

Rich = Closer

Felix = Gatekeeper

Joel = Key Decision Maker

Produto: Scalable Closers/Dragon 100

 

[135:30] All right. So let's do this, okay? So I'm going to be the key decision maker. James or Felix, who wants to be the gatekeeper?

 

Felix: I'll be the gatekeeper.

 

[135:45] Okay, so then Rich, what are you – let's say, are you confident of selling one of the B2B products, maybe Danlock Consulting or HTI or Dragon 100? Or is there a product or service that you're familiar with yourself?

 

[136:00] Rich: Yeah. I'm thinking more like what about closers? Can I do closers? So like having scalable closers, basically close for you?

 

Joel: Yes. Sure.

 

Rich: Okay.

 

[136:15] [RING RING]

 

Felix: Hello, this is Felix speaking.

 

Rich: Hello, Felix. This is Rich Kava. Is Joel available?

 

[136:30] Felix: He is in the office. What's this about?

 

Rich: Yeah, I reached out on LinkedIn. We connected. We've been talking a little bit, and he mentioned he wants to scale the business during this pandemic.

 

[136:45] Felix: Okay, and what do you do?

 

Rich: I help businesses scale by providing them with scalable closers.

 

[137:00] Felix: Okay. And you connected on LinkedIn?

 

Rich: Yes.

 

Felix: All right. Let me see if he's available. Hold on one second.

 

[137:15] [HOLD MUSIC]

 

Felix: Yeah, so he's actually in a meeting right now. Can I take a message?

 

[137:30] Rich: Sure. Can you let him know that Rich from Dragon 100 called? I wanted to follow up on our LinkedIn conversation about scaling his business. My number is 778-555-1234.

 

[137:45] Felix: Okay, got it. I'll let him know.

 

Rich: Thank you so much. Have a great day.

 

Felix: You too. Bye.

 

[138:00] [END OF ROLE PLAY #1]

 

[138:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Role Play #1 Feedback Session"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Sessão de feedback estruturado de múltiplos instrutores (Adam, Felix, Joel, James)

 

[138:30] Joel: Okay. Great. Let's give some feedback. So let's see Adam and Felix, you guys go first. Give some feedback to Rich. Adam, you want to go first?

 

[138:45] Adam: Oh. There you go, Adam. You can go first.

 

Adam: Okay. So first of all, Rich, well done. Good that you held that. You were not too flustered when I gave you that.

 

[139:00] But the first thing is when you directly came in to say, can I speak with James, for me as the gatekeeper in this specific case, that felt like, okay, who is even calling? Like, who are you? Right? It was a little bit more rude now.

 

[139:15] And when you then said like Dragon 100 and then lock, you didn't give me much time to react to that. So for me, it felt in this specific case, it felt to me more as if you were trying to get by me quick and it made me even more alert.

 

[139:30] So when you directly wanted to speak with him. So therefore, when I tried to get you like to the email response, you could have asked me, okay, so what specifically do you want me to write in that email?

 

[139:45] Like, what are you looking for to be able to know if this makes sense or not? Like, how can I make your job easier? Because when I speak to executive gatekeepers, then in this case, like they have a lot to do as we already spoke about, right?

 

[140:00] Like as Joel and Felix and James already laid out very well. I don't have a lot of time. So if you want to make my job easier, I respect that. I like that. I think that's great.

 

[140:15] So therefore, when you ask for processes, for example, like how far do you respond? Like two days felt pushy to me. But if you ask, well, Adam, tell me in your company, like what's the normal process? How do you respond to emails? How do you treat emails? What's your usual response time?

 

[140:30] Then it is less aggressive and it lets me tell you what our times are that you can calculate with that. And from there, you can move on. That's at least how I do it. And it works very well.

 

[140:45] Joel: Great. And I'll stack on that real quick because you touched on it already, Rich. I think something that would really help you when you're in that in your closing, especially talking to Gatekeeper, is using the kind of taking a step back that we shared earlier today in class.

 

[141:00] So if you said something to Adam where it's sort of like, you know, you're getting off on the wrong foot a little bit, Adam's feeling like you're trying to get past him, right? Then it's sort of diffusing the situation, right?

 

[141:15] You could say, hey, you know, Adam, I feel like we're getting off on the wrong foot here. Let me take a step back and can I kind of share a few why I'm calling and why I'm looking for Felix, right?

 

[141:30] Changing the tonality, taking a step back, and then getting Adam, slowing down the pace because Adam is controlling the pace pretty damn fast too, right?

 

[141:45] You take a step back, control it, and ask for permission to continue. And if you do it politely and respectfully, I'm sure Adam would at least let you say, like, Adam, you would let him say at least hear him out, right?

 

[142:00] Adam: Yeah, exactly.

 

Joel: So Adam would hear you out. And then based on that, then it goes into the way you sort of pitch or the way you explain what you're calling about.

 

[142:15] You could then ask more questions to dig more about the organization and what Adam, even what Adam does, right? How long Adam's been there, depending on what rapport you can build with him as well, right?

 

[142:30] Does that make sense, Rich? So Rich, practice a lot of the taking a step back, the different ways to diffuse a situation, really, and then, yeah, slowing down, and you should be good.

 

[142:45] Fantastic. How about Felix? Any feedback for Rich?

 

Felix: Yeah, I wanted to add on to the slowdown part too, right? Like, I think the tonality, overall, it gives me a really fast pace, right?

 

[143:00] It feels like you're trying to rush through the call in some way, which to me, I think the objective is really to slow down and talk to the gatekeeper, right?

 

[143:15] Like, if you feel like there's a really, like, strong gatekeeper in front of you, soften it down a little bit to have a genuine conversation with them. And in order to do that, I think you have to slow down and slow down the pace, which Joel and Adam has already covered, but that's the main feedback that I want to give.

 

[143:30] Joel: Yeah, awesome try, though, Rich. Very good.

 

Rich: Okay, cool. Thank you for your feedback.

 

[143:45] Joel: Yes, and thanks, Adam, as well. Great, great play there.

[147:45] Felix: Okay.

[149:00] Felix: Okay, so what do you guys do exactly?

 

[149:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO (Implícito) ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Building Credibility Through Storytelling"

 

NÚMEROS: $1M mencionado

 

VISUAL: Técnica de storytelling para criar conexão emocional e credibilidade antes de revelar produto

 

[149:30] Desmond: So we work with a gentleman by the name of Dan Lok. Have you heard of Dan Lok before?

 

Felix: I think I've heard the name, but I'm not too familiar.

 

[149:45] Desmond: Okay, so Dan Lok is a very successful entrepreneur. He's built multiple eight-figure businesses. And what he does now is he runs a very exclusive mastermind for AAA players, people who are already doing very well in their businesses.

 

[150:00] And the reason I'm reaching out to Joel is because Dan looked at Joel's profile, saw what he's been doing in the real estate brokerage space, and thought that Joel might be a great fit for this mastermind.

 

[150:15] Felix: Okay, interesting. So this is like a mastermind group?

 

Desmond: Yes, exactly. But here's the thing, Felix. This is not for everyone. In fact, Dan is very selective about who he invites.

 

[150:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO (Implícito) ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Creating Exclusivity & Scarcity"

 

NÚMEROS: AAA players mencionado

 

VISUAL: Posicionamento de exclusividade - "não é para todos", seletividade, inversão de dinâmica de poder

 

[150:45] Desmond (continuação): And I want to be very transparent with you, Felix. I don't know if Joel is even going to be interested in this. That's why I'm calling. I want to see if this is even something that would make sense for him.

 

[151:00] Felix: Okay, that's fair. So what would you need from me?

 

[151:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO (Implícito) ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Positioning Gatekeeper as Partner"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Mudança de dinâmica - gatekeeper como aliado, não obstáculo; "what would you need from me?"

 

[151:30] Desmond: Well, here's what I'm thinking. I know Joel is probably very busy, and I respect that. I also know that you probably protect his time very well, and I respect that too.

 

[151:45] So what I'd like to do is this: I'd like to just pass on some information to Joel. It doesn't have to be right now. Whenever he has a moment, he can take a look at it.

 

[152:00] And if it's something that interests him, great. If not, no worries at all. Does that sound fair?

 

Felix: Yeah, that sounds reasonable. What kind of information are we talking about?

 

[152:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO (Implícito) ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Permission-Based Approach"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Pedindo permissão em cada passo, removendo pressão, criando ambiente colaborativo

 

[152:30] Desmond: So, Felix, before I get into that, can I ask you a quick question? And you don't have to answer if you're not comfortable.

 

Felix: Sure, go ahead.

 

[152:45] Desmond: What would you say is the biggest challenge that Joel is facing right now in the business? Or what's the thing that keeps him up at night?

 

[153:00] Felix: Well, I mean, like any real estate brokerage, it's always about getting more leads, more traffic, more eyeballs on the properties.

 

[153:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO (Implícito) ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Extracting Pain Points from Gatekeeper"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Técnica avançada - conseguir informações valiosas do gatekeeper através de rapport e permissão

 

[153:30] Desmond: Got it. Traffic, leads, eyeballs. That makes total sense. And Felix, I really appreciate you sharing that with me. That's actually very helpful because now I know exactly what to mention to Dan when I talk to him.

 

[153:45] Because here's the thing: Dan has a very strong background in social media and digital marketing. He's helped a lot of businesses scale through those channels.

 

[154:00] So this might actually be perfect for Joel. But again, I don't want to assume anything. I just want to pass the information along and let Joel decide.

 

Felix: Okay, that sounds good. So what do you need me to do?

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Ponto de virada - gatekeeper pergunta "what do you need me to do?" - agora é parceiro ativo

[154:45] Felix: Oh, I appreciate that.

 

Desmond: Absolutely. So here's what I'd like to do. I'm going to give you my contact information. My name is Desmond. D-E-S-M-O-N-D.

NÚMEROS: 778-987-8888

 

VISUAL: Dar informação primeiro, depois pedir; criar obrigação psicológica através de reciprocidade

 

[155:45] Desmond: Now, Felix, since we're doing that, do you mind if I also grab your email? Because any communications, I'm going to make sure I CC you in on everything so that you're in the loop on everything.

 

[156:00] If you don't feel comfortable, just let me know. We protect each other here because you're a gatekeeper, I'm a gatekeeper as well. I don't want to get you in trouble.

 

Felix: 100%, yeah. And yeah, so Joel at abc.com, that's my email.

 

[156:15] Desmond: Joel at, let me write that down. Joel at abc.com. Correct, got it.

 

[156:30] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO (Implícito) ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Identity Alignment Technique"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: "You're a gatekeeper, I'm a gatekeeper" - criar identidade compartilhada, nós vs eles

 

[156:45] Desmond: So what I'll do, Desmond, is I'll just pass this note over to James once he's done. And I'll mention to him how you found, how Dan found him and all that.

 

[157:00] And then, yeah, whatever he lets me know, I'll get back to you as well on that. That sound good?

 

Desmond: Got it. Now, Joel, before we get off the call here, hey, just since I've got you on here right now, what's the most important thing for James right now?

 

[157:15] I want to make you look like a superstar. If I can pass that information on to Dan, when James and Dan, should they choose to connect, I want to make sure that Dan addresses whatever is important to James.

 

[157:30] So what's important for James? And don't have to reveal anything that's not comfortable for you to reveal. What do you think James would benefit from the most right now? And how can that make you a superstar?

 

[157:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO (Implícito) ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Making Gatekeeper the Hero"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: Posicionamento - "make you look like a superstar" - alinhamento de incentivos

 

[158:00] Felix: Well, you know, we're an insurance brokerage. So the biggest thing is obviously having more traffic, more eyes, right? More eyeballs, more people to reach out to. So I guess traffic, list size, things like that. Those are always crucial.

 

[158:15] Desmond: Yep. By the way, what do you guys do actually? I know you guys run a mastermind, but is that your main business?

 

[158:30] Felix: Dan has done a lot of things. You know, I mean, he's killed it. He's really done really well with social media. I don't know if James and him would really enjoy speaking about that.

 

[158:45] Dan, I know, loves to strategize. So maybe him and your boss, James, they might be visionaries. They might strategize on better ways of generating traffic.

 

[159:00] Felix: That's cool. What industry would you guys say you guys are in then?

 

Desmond: Dan has done a lot in social media, but he's also big in acquisitions. So he helps business owners to acquire other companies.

 

[159:15] Felix: Okay, so he's like an investor. Okay, the dealmaker investor. Okay.

 

Desmond: He strategizes with AAA players, and then they work together. Maybe they acquire companies together, right?

 

[159:30] So, but I mean, if your boss is needing some traffic stuff, I won't reveal that, right? I'll make sure just Dan knows that. If we can help out, I'll make sure that you get the credit for it. Does that sound good?

 

[159:45] Felix: Oh yeah, absolutely. And same goes to you as well, right? Thanks for reaching out. I mean, it was sort of random, but it looks like you've got my back and I got your back. I like where this is going. I respect that.

 

[160:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO (Implícito) ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Mutual Alliance Established"

 

NÚMEROS: Não especificados

 

VISUAL: "You got my back, I got your back" - aliança estabelecida, não mais adversários

[161:45] And again, you are in full control here. If you don't think James is going to be impressed or anything, don't do anything to risk your position. But if I can be of service to you and I can help connect the two big guys together, then let's work together, okay?

Desmond: Hey, Joel, appreciate it, man. I'll send you an email. Thank you.
